narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing this path, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid LSAT score and upper-middle family income background. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 47.8% chance of failure for you, which crossed the threshold leading to your application's rejection. This prediction is based on patterns from thousands of past students' academic records, family details, and exam outcomes. The two biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your Year 1 and Year 3 school grade deciles, which placed you in the lower ranges compared to the typical student's mid-range performance. Specifically, your Year 3 decile had the strongest negative pull, suggesting it raised concerns about sustaining strong academic momentum, while your Year 1 decile added to that worry. On a brighter note, your LSAT score stood out as a major positive force, well above the average and pushing the model toward expecting bar exam success. Your undergraduate GPA, though below the typical 3.21 average, still offered a modest helpful nudge in the right direction, as did your upper-middle family income and choice to study full-time, which align with most successful students. Overall, while your LSAT gives you a real edge over many applicants, the model weighed your earlier and later school deciles most heavily, seeing them as bigger risks than your strengths could fully offset. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your profile is stronger in test-taking ability but lags in consistent school rankings, which the model flags as key predictors. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your grade deciles, perhaps seeking tutoring tailored for Black women in law prep, and reapply after building that record; your LSAT positions you well for success with those improvements. We're rooting for you.
